figures.caption: Table 10; PatchTST [Nie et al., 2023] Table 3; DLinear [Zeng et al., 2023] Table 2; RMoK-B [Han et al., 2024] Table 2; TimeKAN [Huang et al., 2025]. First counts use unrounded values; rounded ties may not reproduce counts exactly.
Table 4: Per-dataset hyperparameters. All other parameters are fixed: P=16, S=8, d=32, KAN hidden= 64, depth= 2, G=5, spline order p=3, MA kernel KMA=25.
Table 6: Ablation study (MSE, H=96, single seed=42; values differ from 10-seed means in Table 1). Each row removes or replaces one component from the full DECOMPKAN.
Figure 5: Learned B-spline edge functions on PPG-DaLiA (H=96, 15 sensor channels). Unlike Weather (Figure 4), the PPG model learns predominantly threshold-like latent edge functions.

Figure 6: Mean activation range (maxϕ−minϕ) per KAN layer on Weather, with error bars showing ±1 standard deviation across all edges. Layer 0 (1312 → 64) exhibits structured sparsity with low mean range, while deeper layers are uniformly more active.

Figure 4: Learned B-spline edge functions ϕi→j(x) from the first KAN layer of DECOMPKAN trained on Weather (H=96). Top row: trend branch (blue); bottom row: residual branch (red). Each subplot shows one edge’s activation function over x ∈ [−3, 3]. Edge labels indicate shape classification. The trend branch learns smoother functions; the residual branch learns sharper, more oscillatory transformations.

Figure 2: DECOMPKAN architecture. Input is normalized (RevIN + learned adaptive), decomposed via moving average (KMA=25), and each component is forecast by an independent Patch-KAN branch. Outputs are summed and denormalized. Parameter count varies with L and H (see Table B).
Table 2: Controlled comparison—all models trained with DECOMPKAN’s recipe (Appendix A). Single-seed (seed=42). Red: best. Blue: second. First counts use unrounded values.
